event: Paleo-Indian peoples migrate into North America, with early human settlement reaching what is now Canada via the Bering land bridge and ice-free corridors.
Distinct Indigenous cultures develop across the land as hunting societies adapt to the post-glacial environment.
The Maritime Archaic and Pre-Dorset cultures flourish in the eastern and Arctic regions.
The Dorset culture emerges across the Canadian Arctic, known for distinctive carvings and ivory work.
Norse explorer Leif Erikson establishes a short-lived settlement at L'Anse aux Meadows in Newfoundland, the first known European presence in the Americas.
The Thule people, ancestors of the modern Inuit, spread across the Arctic, displacing the Dorset culture.
John Cabot, sailing for England, explores the Atlantic coast and lays the basis for later English claims to North America.
Jacques Cartier claims the Gaspe Peninsula for France, beginning French exploration of the St. Lawrence region.
Humphrey Gilbert claims Newfoundland for England, establishing one of the earliest English overseas possessions.
Samuel de Champlain helps found Port-Royal in Acadia, an early permanent French settlement.
Samuel de Champlain founds Quebec City, establishing the heart of New France.
Henry Hudson explores Hudson Bay, opening the region to later fur-trade activity.
Ville-Marie, the settlement that becomes Montreal, is founded as a French missionary outpost.
King Charles II charters the Hudson's Bay Company, granting it trading rights over the vast Rupert's Land watershed.
The Treaty of Utrecht cedes Acadia, Newfoundland, and Hudson Bay territories to Britain.
British authorities begin the deportation of the Acadians, expelling thousands of French settlers from the Maritimes.
British forces defeat the French at the Battle of the Plains of Abraham, leading to the fall of Quebec City.
The Treaty of Paris ends the Seven Years' War, transferring New France to Britain, while the Royal Proclamation recognizes Indigenous land rights.
The Quebec Act guarantees French Canadians religious and civil-law rights, helping secure their loyalty during the American Revolution.
Tens of thousands of United Empire Loyalists flee the newly independent United States and settle in British North America.
The Constitutional Act divides Quebec into Upper Canada and Lower Canada, each with its own legislature.
The War of 1812 begins, with American invasions repelled and key battles shaping a sense of Canadian identity.
Slavery is abolished throughout the British Empire, ending legal slavery in British North America.
Rebellions break out in Upper and Lower Canada, demanding responsible government and political reform.
The Act of Union merges Upper and Lower Canada into the single Province of Canada.
Responsible government is achieved in the Province of Canada and Nova Scotia, giving elected assemblies control over domestic affairs.
The British North America Act creates the Dominion of Canada by uniting Ontario, Quebec, New Brunswick, and Nova Scotia.
The Red River Resistance led by Louis Riel asserts Metis rights amid the transfer of Rupert's Land to Canada.
Manitoba enters Confederation, and the Northwest Territories are added to the new nation.
British Columbia joins Confederation after being promised a transcontinental railway.
Prince Edward Island joins Confederation, and the North-West Mounted Police are established.
The Canadian Pacific Railway is completed, and the North-West Rebellion ends with Louis Riel's execution.
The Klondike Gold Rush peaks in the Yukon, which becomes a separate territory.
Alberta and Saskatchewan are created as provinces from the Northwest Territories.
Canada enters World War I alongside Britain, sending hundreds of thousands of troops overseas.
Canadian forces capture Vimy Ridge, a defining moment of national pride, as conscription divides the country.
Women win the right to vote in federal elections following years of suffrage campaigning.
The Statute of Westminster grants Canada full legislative independence in foreign and domestic affairs.
Canada declares war on Germany, joining World War II as an independent nation.
Canadian troops land at Juno Beach during the D-Day invasion of Normandy.
Canada becomes a founding member of the United Nations.
Newfoundland joins Confederation as Canada's tenth province, and Canada becomes a founding member of NATO.
Canada adopts its iconic red-and-white maple leaf national flag.
Canada celebrates its centennial and hosts Expo 67 in Montreal.
The Official Languages Act establishes English and French as Canada's official languages.
The October Crisis erupts as the FLQ carries out kidnappings, prompting the invocation of the War Measures Act in peacetime.
Canada patriates its Constitution and adopts the Charter of Rights and Freedoms, ending British legislative authority.
The Quebec sovereignty referendum narrowly fails, with the federalist side winning by a razor-thin margin.
Nunavut is established as a self-governing Inuit territory carved from the Northwest Territories.
Canada legalizes same-sex marriage nationwide through the Civil Marriage Act.
Prime Minister Stephen Harper issues a formal apology for the residential school system that harmed Indigenous children.
The Truth and Reconciliation Commission releases its final report documenting the legacy of residential schools.
Canada legalizes recreational cannabis nationwide, becoming one of the first major nations to do so.
